    Sprint 0 – Problem Framing, Infrastructure Setup & RAG Foundations

    📄

    Evaluating basic end-to-end retrieval and generation

    Lesson 6

    Introduction


    Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines combine a retrieval phase (fetching relevant documents) with a generation phase (using an LLM to produce answers from those documents). To ensure a RAG system performs well, we must evaluate both components and the end-to-end results. In this lesson we focus on development-time evaluation - utilising offline metrics and test datasets.


    For clarity, here is how development evaluation is different compared to production evaluation. During development, we evaluate the RAG pipeline on a labeled dataset of queries, documents, and reference answers. The goal is to catch issues early by measuring retrieval accuracy and answer quality with automatic metrics. This is different from production evaluation, which monitors real user interactions (e.g. click-through rates, user ratings, A/B tests). In development, metrics provide quick, repeatable feedback to guide model improvements, whereas production eval focuses on user satisfaction and business KPIs.



    Evaluation Stages in a RAG Pipeline


    A basic RAG architecture has two main stages that should be evaluated separately and jointly:

    • Retrieval Stage: Given a user query, the retriever selects a set of relevant text chunks or documents from a knowledge base. We evaluate how well the retriever finds the needed information.

    • Generation Stage: The LLM takes the query and retrieved context to generate an answer. We evaluate the quality of the generated answer (accuracy, relevance, coherence, etc.) in relevance to the question and the provided context.


    By evaluating each stage, we can pinpoint whether errors come from missing or irrelevant context (retriever issues) or from the LLM response (generation issues). In practice, we also evaluate the end-to-end output (i.e. does the final answer correctly and fully answer the user’s question?).



    Evaluating the Retrieval Component




    For the retrieval phase, we typically use information retrieval metrics on an annotated test/reference set (questions with known relevant documents). Common metrics include precision, recall, and ranking-based measures:


    • Precision@K: Out of the top K documents retrieved, the proportion that are relevant to the query. This measures specificity - a high precision@K means most retrieved items were truly useful. For example, precision@5 = 0.6 means that 60% of the top 5 retrieved chunks were relevant. High precision is important when we want mostly correct docs and few distractors in the context. It also helps in tuning the number of documents we want retrieved or how many should be kept after the re-ranking stage.

    • Recall@K: The fraction of all relevant documents that are found in the top K results. This measures coverage - a high recall@K means that the retriever is finding most of the material needed to answer the question. For instance, recall@10 = 0.8 means 80% of all relevant info was retrieved in the top 10. High recall is critical to ensure no necessary facts are missed by the retriever.

    • F1@K: The harmonic mean of precision@K and recall@K. This gives a single score balancing both, useful if we need to trade off between precision and recall for the retrieval. A high F1@K indicates the retriever is both finding most of the right info and not returning too much irrelevant info.

    • Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR): The average of the reciprocal of the rank at which the first relevant document is found for each query. This metric considers the ordering of results. An MRR of 1.0 means every query’s top-ranked result was relevant (ideal), whereas a lower MRR means users might have to sift through more top results to find a relevant one. MRR is especially useful if typically only one document is needed and we care about it appearing at rank 1 or very high.


    In practice, you would choose a suitable @K (e.g. K=5 or 10) based on how many documents your system uses. High recall is often vital in RAG so that the answer has all necessary facts, but precision matters to avoid noise in the context that could confuse the LLM. If the retriever returns too many irrelevant passages, the generation stage might be distracted (or the answer might contain extraneous info). We monitor both precision and recall to ensure the retriever is returning focused yet comprehensive context.



    Retrieval Quality Metrics in Context


    Beyond classical metrics, RAG-specific evaluation can directly measure how useful the retrieved context was for answering the question:


    • Context Relevance: This metric checks whether the retrieved context is relevant to the query and contains mostly information needed for the answer. Ideally, the context should only contain the information necessary to answer. If a lot of unrelated text is included, the context relevance score will be lower. A high context relevance indicates a high signal-to-noise ratio in retrieved docs.

    • Context Recall: This measures whether the retriever found all the information required to answer the question. One way to compute this is to take the reference answer and verify if each fact appears in the retrieved documents. If the answer requires certain key facts that are missing from the context, context recall will be low. High context recall means the retriever didn’t miss any critical pieces.


    These metrics help in making sure that retrieval stage works as efficiently as possible: find all the right information and as little of irrelevant noise as possible.



    Evaluating the Generation Component




    Once relevant documents are retrieved, the LLM continues to generate an answer. The generation stage should be evaluated on multiple aspects: correctness, relevance to the query, fluency, and faithfulness to the source material. There are two wide groups of evaluation scenarios:


    • Reference-based evaluation: If we have ground-truth answers or reference outputs for the query, we can compare the LLM answer to the reference.

    • Reference-free evaluation: If no single correct answer is available (e.g. open-ended questions or no annotated answer), we rely on criteria like factual consistency with context or human/LLM judgment of quality.



    Traditional Reference-Based Metrics


    When a reference answer is available, we can use classic NLP metrics to compare the generated answer to the ground truth:


    • Exact Match and F1: Exact Match simply checks if the model’s answer string matches the correct answer exactly. A softer measure is token-level F1 score, which measures overlap in content words between the answer and reference.

    • BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy): BLEU measures n-gram overlap precision between the generated text and the reference text. It essentially counts how many words or phrases in the model’s answer appear in the reference.A higher BLEU means the answer is lexically closer to the reference answer.

    • ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation): ROUGE is often used in summarization - it measures recall of n-grams against the reference. In QA, ROUGE can check if the model’s answer includes enough of the key terms from the reference. A high ROUGE means the model covered many of the reference output words (good for checking if all points were mentioned). 

      • Note: As a recall metric, ROUGE may reward verbosity, so it should be interpreted alongside precision metrics.


    While reference-based metrics are handy and objective, you need to be careful as open-ended generation has high variability. Two answers can have the same correct info with different wording. Studies have found that simple overlap metrics can correlate poorly with true answer quality.



    Checking Answer Correctness and Factuality


    In many retrieval QA scenarios, the ultimate goal is correct factual answers. If a ground-truth answer is known, a straightforward measure is accuracy (e.g. does the model state the correct fact?). For yes/no or multiple-choice, accuracy is clear. For open questions, one approach is to use an LLM to judge correctness against a reference: for example, the Answer Accuracy metric uses an LLM-as-a-judge to compare the model’s answer to the true answer. In RAG evaluations, we could prompt an LLM to rate how well the answer matches the reference on a scale (e.g. 0 = incorrect, 1 = partially correct, 2 = correct), converting that to a score. This goes beyond exact string match by allowing semantic equivalence.


    Another crucial aspect is factual consistency with the retrieved context, often called faithfulness. We want to ensure the model uses the provided documents and does not hallucinate new information. Useful metric here are:


    • Faithfulness: Measures whether all facts or claims in the generated answer are supported by the retrieved context. The answer is checked against the source chunks - if the model introduces details not present in the context, it gets penalized. One way to implement this (as done in RAGAS) is to break the answer into individual statements and verify each statement against the context using an LLM or string match. The faithfulness score (often 0 to 1) reflects the fraction of answer statements that can be found or inferred from the retrieved documents. A score of 1 means the answer is fully grounded in the supplied evidence. This is critical for detecting hallucinations: even if an answer sounds plausible, if it can’t be grounded in context, the faithfulness metric will be low. In development, you should flag low-faithfulness answers for review (they might be hallucinations or mistakes in retrieval).

      • Note: As discussed in other lessons, you need to know how different evaluation frameworks calculate their metrics. In the example above, RAGAS runs multiple LLM judge calls for a single query, potentially inflating your bill.

    • Answer Relevance and Completeness: Apart from factual correctness, we evaluate if the answer actually addresses the user’s question. An answer might be factually accurate about something in the documents but still not relevant to what was asked. Answer Relevancy is a metric that checks how directly the answer responds to the question. It typically penalizes answers that wander off-topic, include unnecessary info, or only partially answer the question. This can be assessed by LLMs as well. A high answer relevancy score means the answer is on-point and sufficiently detailed for the query, without off-topic or missing pieces. Notably, answer relevancy does not require factual correctness - it’s more about staying on topic and being responsive. So we use it alongside correctness metrics.


    In summary, for the generation stage we track both surface-level quality (fluency, grammar, etc., which modern LLMs generally handle well) and content quality: Is it correct (aligned with ground truth or facts in context)? Is it faithful to provided sources? Is it relevant and complete for the question? These qualities can be quantified with a mix of reference comparisons and model-based evaluations.
